question: $\left( {\frac{{{\rm{Permeability}}}}{{{\rm{Permittivity}}}}} \right)$ will have the dimensional formula of :
explanation:
From $\phi \vec B.d\vec l = {\mu _0}{i_{net}}$
$[{\mu _0}] = \left[ {\frac{{Weber}}{{A - m}}} \right] = \left[ {\frac{{Volt - {\rm{Sec}}}}{{A - m}}} \right] = \left[ {\frac{{(Joul/coul) - {\rm{Sec}}}}{{A - m}}} \right]$
$ = \left[ {\frac{{N - m - {\mathop{\rm Sec}\nolimits} }}{{(A - {\mathop{\rm Sec}\nolimits} )(A - m)}}} \right] = \left[ {\frac{N}{{{A^2}}}} \right] = ML{T^{ - 2}}{A^{ - 2}}$
Also, $\left[ {\frac{1}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}} \right] = \left[ {\frac{{F{r^2}}}{{{Q^2}}}} \right] = \frac{{ML{T^{ - 2}}.{L^2}}}{{{A^2}{T^2}}} = M{L^3}{T^{ - 4}}{A^{ - 2}}$
$\therefore \left[ {\frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}} \right] = {M^2}{L^4}{T^{ - 6}}{A^{ - 4}}.$
subject: Physics

question:
Three of the quantities defined below are the same dimensional formula. Identify them
${\rm{(i)}}\,\,\sqrt {{\rm{Energy/Mass}}} $
${\rm{(ii)}}\,\,\sqrt {{\rm{Pressure/Density}}} $
${\rm{(iii)}}\,\,\sqrt {{\rm{Force/Linear }}\,{\rm{mass }}\,{\rm{density}}} $
${\rm{(iv)}}\,\,\sqrt {{\rm{Angular}}\,\,{\rm{frequency/radius}}} $
explanation:
$\left[ {\frac{{{\rm{Energy}}}}{{{\rm{Mass}}}}} \right]$ $ = \left[ {\frac{{M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}}}{M}} \right] = [{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}]$
$\left[ {\frac{{{\rm{Pressure}}}}{{{\rm{Density}}}}} \right]$ $ = \left[ {\frac{{M{L^{ - 1}}{T^{ - 2}}}}{{M{L^{ - 3}}}}} \right] = [{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}]$
$\left[ {\frac{{{\rm{Force}}}}{{{\rm{Linear}}\,\,{\rm{mass}}\,\,{\rm{density}}}}} \right]$$ = \left[ {\frac{{ML{T^{ - 2}}}}{{M{L^{ - 1}}}}} \right] = [{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}]$
subject: Physics

question: In the relation  $y = r\sin \left( {\omega t - kx} \right),$ the dimensional formula of  $\omega /k$ are
explanation:
Given, $y = r\sin \left( {\omega t - hx} \right)$
where, $\omega t = angle$
$\therefore \,\omega = \frac{1}{T} = \left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
($\because $ angle of dimensionless)
Similarly $kx = $ angle
$\therefore k = \frac{1}{x} = \left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
$\therefore \frac{\omega }{k} = \frac{{\left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^{ - 1}}} \right]}} = \left[ {L{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following pairs of physical quantities do not have same dimensional formula?
explanation:
Because, dimension formula of tension same as force
$ = [ML{T^{ - 2}}]$
and surface tension ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{force}}}}{{{\rm{length}}}}$ $ = [M{L^0}{T^{ - 2}}]$
Work and torque, both are product of force and length.
Impulse is equal to change in momentum.
subject: Physics

question: One mole of an ideal gas at standard temperature and pressure occupies 22.4 $L$ (molar volume). The ratio of molar volume to the atomic volume of a mole of hydrogen? (Take the size of hydrogen molecule to be about $1\mathop A\limits^ \circ  $)
explanation:
Given, molar of one mole of hydrogen
$ = 22.4L = 22.4 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^3}$
Diameter of hydrogen molecule $(r) = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 10}}}}{2}$
$ = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 10}}m$
Volume of one molecule of hydrogen $ = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}$
$\frac{4}{3} \times 3.14 \times {\left( {0.5 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)^3}$
$ = 5.234 \times {10^{ - 31}}{m^3}$
Number of molecules in one mole of hydrogen
$ = $ Avogadro number ($N$)
$ = 6.023 \times {10^{23}}$
$\therefore $ Atomic volume of one mole of hydrogen $ = $ Number of molecules in one mole of hydrogen $ \times $ Volume of one molecule of hydrogen
$ = 6.023 \times {10^{23}} \times 5.234 \times {10^{31}}$
$ = 3.152 \times {10^{ - 7}}{m^3}$
Now, $\frac{{{\rm{Molar}}\,\,{\rm{volume}}}}{{{\rm{Atomic}}\,\,{\rm{volume}}}}$ $ = \frac{{22.4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{3.154 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} = 7.1 \times {10^4}$
subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Units and Dimensions
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Units and Dimensions

question: The photograph of a house occupies an area of $1.75\,c{m^2}$ on a 35 $mm$ slide. The slide is projected on to a screen and the area of the house on the screen is $1.55\,\,{m^2}.$ The linear magnification of the projector-screen arrangement is
explanation:
Area of object $ = 1.7c{m^2} = 1.75 \times {10^{ - 4}}{m^2}$
Area of image $ = 1.55{m^2}$
$\therefore \,{\rm{Area}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{magnification = }}\frac{{{\rm{Area}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{image}}}}{{{\rm{Area}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{object}}}}$
$ = \frac{{1.55}}{{1.75 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}} = 8857$
Linear magnification ${\rm{ = }}\sqrt {{\rm{Area}}\,\,{\rm{magnification}}} $
$ = \sqrt {8857} = 94.1$
subject: Physics

question: A student measures the thickness of a human hair by looking at it through a microscope of magnification 100. He makes 20 observations and finds that the average width of the hair in the field of view of the microscope is 3.5 $mm$. The thickness of hair is
explanation:
Magnification of microscope $ = 100$
Observed width of the hair $ = 3.5\,mm$
${\rm{Magnification = }}\frac{{{\rm{Observed}}\,\,{\rm{width}}}}{{{\rm{Real}}\,\,{\rm{width}}}}$
${\rm{Realwidth = }}\frac{{{\rm{Observed}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{width}}}}{{{\rm{magnification}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{3}}{\rm{.5}}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}$
$ = 0.035\,mm$
subject: Physics

Method of dimensions distinguish between the physical quantities having same dimensions.

Special functions such as trigonometric, logarithmic and exponential functions have dimensions.
The ratio of similar physical quantities, such as angle and refractive index, has no dimensions.

The arguments of special functions, such as the trigonometric, logarithmic and exponential functions must be dimensionless.
The ratio of similar physical quantities, such as angle as the ratio (length/length), refractive index as the ratio of (speed of light in vacuum/speed of light in medium), etc. has no dimensions. So option (2) is correct.

question: A physical quantity of the dimensions length that can be formed out of $c$, $G$ and $\frac{{{e^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}$ is [$c$ is velocity of light, $G$ is universal constant of gravitation and $e$ is charge]
explanation:
$\left[ L \right] = {\left[ c \right]^a}{\left[ G \right]^b}{\left[ {\frac{{{e^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right]^C}$
$\left[ L \right] = {\left[ {L{T^{ - 1}}} \right]^a}{\left[ {{M^{ - 1}}{L^3}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^b}{\left[ {M{L^3}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^C}$
$\left[ L \right] = {L^{a + 3b + 3c}}{M^{ - b + c}}{T^{ - a - 2b - 2c}}$
$a + 3b + 3c = 1$
$ - b + c = 0$
$a + 2b + 2c = 0$
On solving,
$a = - 2,b = \frac{1}{2},c = \frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore L = \frac{1}{{{c^2}}}{\left[ {G.\frac{{{e^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right]^{\frac{1}{2}}}$
subject: Physics

question: The dimensions of 'resistance are same as those of_____where $h$ is the Planck's constant, $e$ is the charge.
explanation:
Resistance $ = \frac{{Potential{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,difference}}{{Current}}$

also $\left[ {\frac{h}{{{e^2}}}} \right]\, = \,\frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {AT} \right]}^2}}}\, = \,[M{L^2}{T^{ - 3}}{A^{ - 2}}]\,$

explanation:

$\therefore [C{V^2}] = [U] = [{M^1}{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}{A^0}]$

question: Which one of the following is NOT correct?
explanation:
Thermal conductivity
${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{Heat}}\,{\text{energy}}\, \times \,{\text{thickness}}}}{{{\text{Area}}\, \times \,{\text{Temperature}}\, \times \,{\text{time}}}}$
$[K] = \frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]\left[ L \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^2}} \right]\left[ K \right]\left[ T \right]}}\, = \,\left[ {{M^1}{L^1}{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 1}}} \right]$
Potential $ = \frac{{Work}}{{Charge}}$
$[V] = \frac{{M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}}}{{\left[ {AT} \right]}}\, = \,\left[ {{M^1}{L^2}{T^{ - 3}}{A^{ - 1}}} \right]$
Permeability of free space
${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{force}}\, \times \,{\text{distance}}}}{{{\text{current}}\, \times \,{\text{current}}\, \times \,{\text{length}}}}$
$\left[ {{\mu _0}} \right]\, = \,\frac{{\left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]\left[ L \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]\left[ A \right]\left[ L \right]}}\, = \,\left[ {{M^1}{L^1}{T^{ - 2}}{A^{ - 2}}} \right]$
$RC$ is the time constant of $RC$ circuit.
$[RC] = [{M^0}{L^0}{T^1}]$

options.text: Dimensional formula of thermal conductivity ($K$) is ${M^1}{L^1}{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 1}}$
Dimensional formula of permeability of free space $({\mu _0})$ is ${M^1}{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}{A^{ - 2}}$

question: The dimensional formula of physical quantity is ${M^a}{L^b}{T^c}$. Then that physical quantity is
explanation:

$\therefore \,\,a = 0,b = 0,c = - 1$

question: The ratio of the dimensions of Planck's constant and that of moment of inertia has the dimensions of
explanation:
Planck's constant ($h$) ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{energy}}}}{{{\rm{frequency}}}}$
$\therefore \,\,[h] = \frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right]}}\, = \,\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
moment of inertia $(I) = $ mass (radius of gyration)$^2$
$[I] = [M][{L^2}] = [M{L^2}{T^0}]$
$\left[ {\frac{{\left[ h \right]}}{{\left[ I \right]}}} \right]\, = \,\frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^0}} \right]}}\, = \,\left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right]\, = \,\left[ {\operatorname{frequency} } \right]$
subject: Physics

subject: Physics

question: If $P$, $Q$ and $R$ are physical quantities having different dimensions, which of the following  combinations can never be a meaningful quantity?
explanation: Physical quantities of different dimensions can not be added or subtracted.

question: The dimensions of Stefan’s constant are
explanation:
Stefan’s constant is given by $\sigma = \frac{E}{{At{\theta ^4}}}$
Where $E$ is energy radiated, $A$ is surface area of body, $t$ is time and $\theta $ is the absolute temperature.
Dimensions of Stefan’s constant
$ = \frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^2} \times T \times {K^4}} \right]}}$
$ = \left[ {M{L^{^0}}{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 4}}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: In the expression for Boyle’s law, the product $PV$ has dimensions of
explanation:
Dimensions of
$PV = \left[ {M{L^{ - 1}}{T^{ - 2}}} \right] \times \left[ {{L^3}} \right] = \left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
Dimensions of force $ = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
Dimensions of impluse $ = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
Dimensions of energy $ = \left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
Dimensions of momentum $ = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
Hence (3) is the correct answer.
subject: Physics

question: The dimensions of torque are same as that of
explanation:
Torque is expressed as
$\tau = $ force ($F$) $ \times $ perpendicular distance ($r$)
Hence, $\left[ \tau \right] = [F] \times [r] = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]\left[ L \right]$
$ = \left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
(1) Dimension of moment of force
$ = \left[ {force} \right] \times \left[ {\operatorname{distance} } \right] = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right] \times \left[ {{L^1}} \right] = \left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
(2) Dimension of pressure $ = \left[ {Force} \right] \times {\left[ {Area} \right]^{ - 1}}$
$ = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right] \times \left[ {{L^{ - 2}}} \right] = \left[ {M{L^{ - 1}}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
(3) Dimension of acceleration $ = \left[ {{M^0}{L^1}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
(4) Dimension of impulse $ = \left[ {\operatorname{force} } \right] \times [\operatorname{time} ]$
$ = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right] \times \left[ {{T^1}} \right] = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
Therefore, the option (1) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: Dimensions of Gyromagnetic ratio are
explanation:
Gyromagnetic ratio is the ratio of magnetic dipole moment of revolving electron to its angular momentum,
i.e., $\frac{\mu }{L} = \frac{e}{{2{m_e}}} = $ gyromagnetic ratio
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} \left[ {\frac{\mu }{L}} \right] = \frac{{\left[ {current \times time} \right]}}{{\left[ {mass} \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {I \times T} \right]}}{{\left[ M \right]}} = [{L^0}{M^{ - 1}}{T^1}{I^1}]$
subject: Physics

question: The force ‘$F$’ acting on a body of density ‘$d$’ are related by the relation $F = \frac{y}{{\sqrt d }}$ . The dimensions of ‘ $y$’ are
explanation:
The dimensions of force $[F] = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
Density $(d) = \left[ {M{L^{ - 3}}{T^0}} \right]$
From the given relation , $F = \frac{y}{{\sqrt d }}$
$ \Rightarrow y = F\sqrt d $
Substituting the above dimensions, we get
$\left[ y \right] = \left[ F \right]{\left[ d \right]^{1/2}} = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]{\left[ {M{L^{ - 3}}{T^0}} \right]^{1/2}}$
$ = \left[ {{M^{\frac{3}{2}}}{L^{^ - \frac{1}{2}}}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: The dimensions of self or mutual inductance are given as
explanation:
Using the expression for energy stored in an inductor,
$U = \frac{1}{2}L{I^2},$
dimensions of mutual inductance or self inductance can be given by
$\left[ L \right] = \frac{{\left[ U \right]}}{{\left[ {{I^2}} \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{I^2}} \right]}} = \left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}{I^{ - 2}}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: The $SI$ unit and dimensions of Stefan’s constant $\sigma $ in case of Stefan’s law of radiation is
explanation:
According to Stefan’s law,
$E = \sigma At{T^4}$
Where, $E = $ energy emitted
$A = $ area
$t = $ time
$T = $ absolute temperature in Kelvin
and $\sigma = $ Stefan’s constant
$ \Rightarrow \sigma = \frac{E}{{At{T^4}}}$
$\therefore $ Unit of Stefan’s constant
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{J/{m^2}s}}{{{K^4}}} = J/{m^2}s{K^4}$
Dimensions of Stefan’s constant
${\rm{\sigma = }}\frac{{\left[ {{\rm{Energy}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{\rm{Area}}} \right]{\rm{ \times }}\left[ {{\rm{Time}}} \right]{\rm{ \times }}{{\left[ {{\rm{Temperature}}} \right]}^{\rm{4}}}}}$
$ = \frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^2} \times T \times {K^4}} \right]}} = \left[ {{M^1}{L^0}{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 4}}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: In case of dimensions of electric field and electric dipole moment, the powers of mass are respectively,
explanation:
Electric field, $E = \frac{F}{q}$
Where, $F = $ force and $q = $ electric charge
Dimension of $E$
$[E] = \frac{{\left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {IT} \right]}} = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 3}}{I^{ - 1}}} \right]$
$\therefore $ Power of mass $ = 1$
Electric dipole moment, $\mu = iA$
Where $i = $ current and $A = $ area
Dimension of $\mu = \left[ {I{L^2}} \right] = \left[ {{M^0}{L^2}{T^0}I} \right]$
Hence, power of mass $ = 0$
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following is the dimensional formula for electric polarisation?
explanation:
Electric polarisation ($P$) is given by
$P = {\varepsilon _0}\chi E$
Where, $E$ is electric field,${\varepsilon _0}$ is the vacuum permittivity, and $\chi $ is electric susceptibility which is a dimensionless quantity.
$ \Rightarrow \left[ P \right] = \left[ {{\varepsilon _0}} \right] \times \left[ E \right]$
$ = \left[ {{M^{ - 1}}{L^{ - 3}}{T^4}{I^2}} \right]\left[ {ML{T^{ - 3}}{I^{ - 1}}} \right] = \left[ {{M^0}{L^{ - 2}}{T^1}{I^1}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: $\left[ {{L^2}{M^1}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$ are the dimensions of
explanation:
Torque $=$ Force $ \times $ Perpendicular distance
i.e., $\tau = F \times r$
$\therefore $ Dimensional formula $\left[ \tau \right] = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]\left[ L \right]$
$ = \left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
Hence (1) is the correct option.
subject: Physics

question: Let Force $F = A\sin (Ct) + B\cos (Dx)$, where $x$ and $t$ are displacment and time respectively,. The dimension of  $\frac{C}{D}$ are same as dimension of
explanation:
Given Force $F = A\sin (Ct) + B\,\cos (Dx)$
Here, ($Ct$ ) and ($Dx$) are dimensionless as they represent angles
$\therefore \left[ C \right] = \frac{1}{{\left[ t \right]}} = \left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
and $\left[ D \right] = \frac{1}{{\left[ x \right]}} = \left[ {{L^{ - 1}}} \right]$
Dimension of $\frac{{\left[ C \right]}}{{\left[ D \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^{ - 1}}} \right]}} = \left[ {L{T^{ - 1}}} \right]$
This is same as the dimension of velocity
subject: Physics

question: Let $\sigma $ and $b$ be Stefan’s constant and Wien’s constant respectively, then dimensions of $\sigma $ $b$ are
explanation:
Dimensions of Stefan’s constant
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\left[ {{\rm{Energy}}{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\rm{per}}{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\rm{unit}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{time}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{\rm{Area}}} \right]{\rm{ \times }}{{\left[ {{\rm{Temperature}}} \right]}^{\rm{4}}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \left[ \sigma \right] = \frac{{\left[ {\mathop u\limits^{} } \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]{{\left[ T \right]}^4}}} = \frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]/\left[ T \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^2}{K^4}} \right]}} = \left[ {M{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 4}}} \right]$
Dimensions of Wien’s constant
${\rm{ = [Wave}}\,\,{\rm{length] \times [Temperature]}}$
$ \Rightarrow \left[ b \right] = \left[ \lambda \right]\left[ T \right] = \left[ {LK} \right]$
$\therefore \,\left[ {\sigma b} \right] = \left[ {M{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 4}}} \right]\left[ {LK} \right] = \left[ {{L^1}{M^1}{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 3}}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: Let $x = \pi R\left( {\frac{{{P^2} - {Q^2}}}{2}} \right)$ , where $P$, $Q$, $R$ are lengths. The physical quantity of $x$ is
explanation:
Given $x = \pi R\left( {\frac{{{P^2} - {Q^2}}}{2}} \right)$
where $P$, $Q$ and $R$ are lengths.
$ \Rightarrow $ Dimensions of $x$ are $[x] = \left[ {\text{L}} \right] \times \left[ {{{\text{L}}^2}} \right] = \left[ {{{\text{L}}^3}} \right]$.
$\therefore \,x$ represents the physical quantity of volume.
subject: Physics

question: The error in the measurement of resistance, when ($10 \pm 0.5$) A current passing through it produces a potential difference of $(100 \pm 6) V$ across it is
explanation:
Since $R=\dfrac{V}{I}=V I^{-1}$ so, maximum error in the measurement of resistance
$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100=\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \times 100+\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \times 100$
Given current $=(10 \pm 0.5) A$ and potential difference $=(100 \pm 6) V$
$\therefore$ Maximum error in the measurement of resistance
$\frac{{\Delta R}}{R} \times 100 = \frac{6}{{100}} \times 100 + \frac{{0.5}}{{10}} \times 100$
$ = 6 + 5 = 11\% $
subject: Physics

question: The internal and external diameters of a hollow cylinder measured with vernier calipers are $(5.73 \pm 0.01)cm$ and $(6.01 \pm 0.01)cm$ respectively. Then the thickness of the cylinder wall is
explanation:
For hollow cylinder.
$d=(5.73 \pm 0.01) c m, D=(6.01 \pm 0.01) c m$
$\therefore$ Thickness, $t=\dfrac{D-d}{2}$
$=\left(\dfrac{6.01-5.73}{2}\right) \pm\left(\dfrac{0.01+0.01}{2}\right)$
$=(0.14 \pm 0.01) c m$
subject: Physics

question: A capacitor of capacitance $(4.0 \pm 0.2) \mu F$ is charged to a potential of $(10.0 \pm 0.1) V$. The charge on the capacitor is
explanation:
$C=(4.0 \pm 0.2) \mu F, V=(10.0 \pm 0.1) V$.
The charge on capacitor is
$Q=C V=4 \times 10=40 \mu C$
Also, $\dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q}=\dfrac{\Delta C}{C}+\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=\dfrac{0.2}{4}+\dfrac{0.1}{10}=0.06$
$\therefore \quad \% \Delta Q=\dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q} \times 100=0.06 \times 100=6 \%$
$\therefore \quad$ Charge can be written as
$(Q \pm \Delta Q \%)=40 \mu C \pm 6 \%$
subject: Physics

question: In a simple pendulum experiment for the determination of acceleration due to gravity, the error in the measurement of the length of the pendulum is $1 \%$ and the error in the measurement of the time period is $2 \%$. The error in the estimation of acceleration due to gravity is
explanation:
In simple pendulum experiment,
$\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}=1 \%, \dfrac{\Delta T}{T}=2 \%$
$\therefore$ Time period, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \Rightarrow g=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} l}{T^{2}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta g}{g}=\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}+2 \dfrac{\Delta T}{T}=1 \%+2(2 \%)=5 \%$
subject: Physics

question: A physical quantity $X$ is given by $X=\dfrac{2 k^{3} l^{2}}{m \sqrt{n}}$. The percentage errors in the measurements of $k, l, m$ and $n$ are $1 \%, 2 \%, 3 \%$ and $4 \%$ respectively. The value of $X$ is uncertain by
explanation:
Given, $X=\dfrac{2 k^{3} l^{2}}{m \sqrt{n}}$ error percentage
$k=1 \%, l=2 \%, m=3 \%, n=4 \%$
Total percentage error in $X$
$\dfrac{\Delta X}{X} \times 100=3\left(\dfrac{\Delta k}{k}\right) \times 100+2\left(\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}\right) \times 100$
$+\left(\dfrac{\Delta m}{m} \times 100\right)+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta n}{n}\right) \times 100$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta X}}{X} \times 100$
$ = 3 \times 1\% + 2 \times 2\% + 3\% + \frac{1}{2} \times 4\% $
$=3+4+3+2=12 \%$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A physical quantity $S$ is related to four observables $a,b,c$ and $d$ as $S=\dfrac{\sqrt{a b}}{c^{3} d^{4}}$. If the percentage errors of measurement in $a$, $b, c$ and $d$ are $2 \%, 1 \%, 1 \%$ and $1 \%$ respectively, then the percentage error in the quantity $S$ is approximately
subject: Physics

question: Consider a physical quantity $Z$ expressed as $Z=\dfrac{A B^{1 / 2}}{C^{2}}$. If the relative error in the magnitudes of $A, B$ and $C$ is $1 \%$, then the relative error in $Z$ will be
explanation:
Given, $Z=\dfrac{A B^{1 / 2}}{C^{2}}$
Now, % relative error will be
$\dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z} \times 100=\dfrac{\Delta A}{A} \%+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta B}{B} \%+\dfrac{2 \Delta C}{C} \%$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z} \times 100=1 \%+\dfrac{1}{2} \%+2 \%=3.5 \%$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a series of measurements of the length of a box in an experiment. The readings are $2.4\,m,2.5\,m,2.6\,m,2.8\,m,3.0\,m$. What would be the relative error?
explanation:
Given that, number of observations,$n=5$
$a_{1}=2.4 m, a_{2}=2.5 m, a_{3}=2.6 m$,$a_{4}=2.8 m, a_{5}=3.0 m$
Mean value of observations,
$\bar{a}=\dfrac{a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}+a_{5}}{5}$
$\bar{a}=\dfrac{2.4+2.5+2.6+2.8+3.0}{5}=\dfrac{13.3}{5}=2.66$
Absolute errors in individual observed value
$\left|\Delta a_{1}\right|=\left|a_{1}-\bar{a}\right|=|2.4-2.66|=|-0.26|=0.26$
$\left|\Delta a_{2}\right|=\left|a_{2}-\bar{a}\right|=|2.5-2.66|=|-0.16|=0.16$
$\left|\Delta a_{3}\right|=\left|a_{3}-\bar{a}\right|=|2.6-2.66|=|-0.06|=0.06$
$\left|\Delta a_{4}\right|=\left|a_{4}-\bar{a}\right|=|2.8-2.66|=0.14$
$\left|\Delta a_{5}\right|=\left|a_{5}-\bar{a}\right|=|3.0-2.66|=0.34$
Mean absolute error,
$\Delta \bar{a}=\dfrac{\left|\Delta a_{1}\right|+\left|\Delta a_{2}\right|+\left|\Delta a_{3}\right|+\left|\Delta a_{4}\right|+\left|\Delta a_{5}\right|}{n}$
$\Delta \bar{a}=\dfrac{0.26+0.16+0.06+0.14+0.34}{5}=\dfrac{0.96}{5}$
$\Delta \bar{a}=0.192$
Relative error $=\dfrac{\text { Mean absolute error }}{\text { Mean value }}$
$=\dfrac{\Delta \bar{a}}{\bar{a}}=\dfrac{0.192}{2.66}=0.072$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: The error in the measurement of the length and the breadth of a rectangular table is $1 \%$. If the length and breadth of the table are $1\,m$ and $50\,cm$ respectively, then the area of the table including error is
explanation:
Area of rectangle, $A=l \times b$
$\ln A=\ln l+\ln b \Rightarrow \dfrac{\delta A}{A}=\dfrac{\delta l}{l}+\dfrac{\delta b}{b}$
Here, $\dfrac{\delta l}{l}=\dfrac{\delta b}{b}=1 \%=0.01$
$\delta A=A\left(\dfrac{\delta l}{l}+\dfrac{\delta b}{b}\right) \quad[\because A=l \times b=1 \times 0.5]$
$=1 \times 0.5(0.01+0.01)=0.01$
Area of the table including error is
$A^{\prime}=A \pm \delta A$
$ \Rightarrow {A^\prime } = (0.5 \pm 0.01){m^2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: If absolute error is $0.05\,m$ for a measured length of $5\,m$. What is the percentage error?
explanation:
Given, absolute error in length,
$\Delta l = 0.05\,m$
Measured length, $l = 5\,m$
Now % error in length $=\dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \times 100$
$=\dfrac{0.05}{5} \times 100$
$=1 \%$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: In five successive measurements, the mass of a ball is measured to be $2.40\,g,2.61\,g$, $2.58\,g,2.73\,g$ and $2.80\,g$. The absolute error in the measurement is
explanation:
Mean mass of the ball is
$\bar{M}=\dfrac{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}+m_{4}+m_{5}}{5}$
$=\dfrac{2.61+2.58+2.40+2.73+2.80}{5}$
$\bar{M}=2.624 \mathrm{~g} \simeq 2.62 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore$ Mean absolute error,
$\Delta \bar{M}=\dfrac{\left(\begin{array}{l}|2.62-2.61|+|2.62-2.58|+ \\ |2.62-2.40|+|2.62-2.73|+ \\ |2.62-2.80|\end{array}\right)}{5} g$
$=\dfrac{0.01+0.04+0.22+0.11+0.18}{5} g$
$ = \frac{{0.56}}{5} = 0.112 \simeq 0.11\,g$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: In an experiment the angles are required to be measured using an instrument in which 29 divisions of the main scale exactly coincide with the 30 divisions of the vernier scale. If the smallest division of the main scale is half a degree $\left(=0.5^{\circ}\right)$, then the least count of the instrument is
explanation:
From the given information,
$\Rightarrow 29 M S D=30 V S D \Rightarrow 1 V S D=\dfrac{29}{30} \times 1 M S D$
$\Rightarrow 1 V S D=\dfrac{29}{30}\left(0.5^{\circ}\right) \quad\left[\because 1 M S D=0.5^{\circ}\right]$
$ \Rightarrow 1\,{\mkern 1mu} VSD=$ $\left(\dfrac{29}{60}\right)^{\circ}$
$\therefore$ Least count of vernier caliper is given as
$L C=1 M S D-1 V S D$
$=0.5^{\circ}-\left(\dfrac{29}{60}\right)^{\circ}$
$=\left(\dfrac{30-29}{60}\right)^{\circ}=\left(\dfrac{1}{60}\right)^{\circ}$
$=1 \min \quad\left[\because 1 \min =\dfrac{1}{60}\right.$ degree $]$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A quantity $z$, to be estimated has a dependency on the variables $a, b$ and $c$ as $z=a b^{2} c^{-2}$. The percentage of error in the measurement of $a, b$ and $c$ are respectively, $2.1 \%, 1.3 \%$ and $2.2 \%$. The percentage of error in the measurement of $z$ would then be
explanation:
Given, $z=a b^{2} c^{-2}$
%Error in $z$ is given by
$\dfrac{\Delta z}{z} \%=\dfrac{\Delta a}{a} \%+\dfrac{2 \Delta b}{b} \%+\dfrac{2 \Delta c}{c} \%$
$=2.1 \%+2(1.3 \%)+2(2.2 \%)=9.1 \%$
So,the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A current carrying conductor obeys Ohm's law $(V=R I)$. If the current passing through the conductor is $I=(5 \pm 0.2) A$ and voltage developed is $V=(60 \pm 6) V$, then find the percentage of error in resistance, $R$
explanation:
Here,$I=(5 \pm 0.2) A \Rightarrow \Delta I=0.2 A$
Voltage developed,
$V=(60 \pm 6) V, \Rightarrow \Delta V=6 V$
By Ohm's law,
$V=R I \Rightarrow R=\dfrac{V}{I}$
Relative error in the Resistance is,
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}=\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}+\dfrac{\Delta I}{I}=\dfrac{6}{60}+\dfrac{0.2}{5}=0.1+0.04$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}=0.14$
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100=0.14 \times 100=14 \%$
So,the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A wooden cubical block of mass, $m = 20\,kg$ is measured within an error of $10\,g$. Its side length, $l = 100\,cm$ is measured within an error of $1\,mm$. Then, the relative error in the measurement of its density is
explanation:
Density $=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }} \Rightarrow \rho=\dfrac{m}{l^{3}}$
So, relative error in density is
$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}=\dfrac{\Delta m}{m}+\dfrac{3 \Delta l}{l}$
Here, $m = 20\,kg,\Delta m = 10\,g = 10 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg$,
$l = 100\,cm,\Delta l = 1\,mm = 0.1\,cm$.
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}=\dfrac{10^{-2}}{20}+\dfrac{3 \times 0.1}{100}$
$=3.5 \times 10^{-3}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question:
A physical quantity $P$ is related to four observations $a, b, c$ and $d$ as follows:
$P=a^{3} b^{2} / c \sqrt{d}$ The percentage errors of measurement in $a, b, c$ and $d$ are $1 \%, 3 \%, 2 \%$, and $4 \%$ respectively. The percentage error in the quantity $P$ is
explanation:
Maximum $\%$ error in
$P = \frac{{\Delta P}}{P} \times 100$
$ = 3\left( {\frac{{\Delta a}}{a} \times 100} \right) + 2\left( {\frac{{\Delta b}}{b} \times 100} \right)$
$+\left(\dfrac{\Delta c}{c} \times 100\right)+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta d}{d} \times 100\right)$
$=3 \times(1)+2 \times(3)+(2)+\dfrac{1}{2} \times(4)=13 \%$
subject: Physics

question: Consider the diameter of a spherical object being measured with the help of a Vernier callipers. Suppose its 10 Vernier Scale Divisions (V.S.D.) are equal to its 9 Main Scale Divisions (M.S.D.). The least division in the M.S. is $0.\,cm$ and the zero of V.S. is at $x = 0.1\,cm$ when the jaws of vernier callipers are closed. If the main scale reading for the diameter is $M = 5\,cm$ and the number of coinciding vernier division is 8, the measured diameter after zero error correction, is
explanation:
Least count $=1 M S D-1 V S D$
$=1 M S D-\dfrac{9}{10} M S D=\dfrac{1}{10} M S D$
$ = \frac{1}{{10}} \times 0.1\,cm = 0.01\,cm$
Zero error $ = + 0.1\,cm$
Main scale reading $ = 5\,cm$
Vernier scale reading $ = 8 \times 0.01 = 0.08\,cm$
Final measurement of diameter
$ = 5 + 0.08 - 0.1 = 4.98\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The percentage errors in quantities $P, Q, R$ and S are $0.5 \%, 1 \%, 3 \%$ and $1.5 \%$ respectively in the measurement of a physical quantity  $A=\dfrac{P^{3} Q^{2}}{\sqrt{R} S}$. The maximum percentage error in the value of $A$ will be:
explanation:
$\frac{{\Delta A}}{A} \times 100 = $
$\left( {\frac{{3\Delta P}}{P} + \frac{{2\Delta Q}}{Q} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\Delta R}}{R} + \frac{{\Delta S}}{S}} \right) \times 100$
$ = 3 \times 0.5 + 2 \times 1 + \frac{1}{2} \times 3 + 1.5$
$\frac{{\Delta A}}{A} \times 100 = 6.5\,\% $
subject: Physics

question: The relative error in the determination of the surface area of a sphere is $\alpha$. Then the relative error in the determination of its volume is:
explanation:
The relative error in surface area is
$\dfrac{\Delta s}{s}=2 \times \dfrac{\Delta r}{r}$
The relative error in volume is
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=3 \times \dfrac{\Delta r}{r} \\& \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=\dfrac{3}{2} \alpha\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: If the percentage errors in measuring the length and the diameter of a wire are $0.1 \%$ each. The percentage error in measuring its resistance will be
explanation:
Percentage error in length and diameter measurement is $0.1 \%$.
Resistance of wire, $R=\dfrac{\rho l}{A}=\dfrac{4 \rho l}{\pi d^{2}}$
Using error analysis
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100 \%=\dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \times 100 \%+2 \dfrac{\Delta d}{d} \times 100 \% \\& =0.1 \%+2 \times 0.1 \%=0.3 \%\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A physical quantity $Q$ is found to depend on quantities $a, b, c$ by the relation $Q=\dfrac{a^{4} b^{3}}{c^{2}}$. The percentage error in $a, b$ and $c$ are $3 \%, 4 \%$ and $5 \%$ respectively. Then, the percentage error in $Q$ is
explanation:
Given : $Q=\dfrac{a^{4} b^{3}}{c^{2}} ; a \rightarrow 3 \%, b \rightarrow 4 \%, c \rightarrow 5 \%$
So, using error analysis, we can calculate the error as
$\dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q} \times 100 \%=\dfrac{4 \Delta a}{a} \times 100 \%+\dfrac{3 \Delta b}{b} \times 100 \%+$
$\dfrac{2 \Delta c}{c} \times 100 \%$
$=4 \times 3+3 \times 4+2 \times 5=34 \%$
subject: Physics

question: The resistance $R=\dfrac{V}{I}$ where $V=(200 \pm 5) V$ and $I=(20 \pm 0.2)$  $A$, the percentage error in the measurement of $R$ is
explanation:
As, $R=\dfrac{V}{I}$
To find $\%$ error in $R$;
$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100 \%=\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \times 100 \%+\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \times 100 \%$
$=\dfrac{5}{200} \times 100+\dfrac{0.2}{20} \times 100=3.5 \%$
subject: Physics

question: To find the spring constant $(k)$ of a spring experimentally, a student commits $2 \%$ positive error in the measurement of time and $1 \%$ negative error in measurement of mass. The percentage error in determining value of $k$ is
explanation:
Time period of a spring - mass system ,
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$
where $m$ is the mass of spring and $k$ is the spring constant.
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta T}{T} \times 100 \%=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta k}{k} \times 100 \%+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} \times 100 \%$
or $2=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{\Delta k}{k} \times 100 \%-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1$
or $2=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{\Delta k}{k} \times 100 \%-1\right]$
or $\dfrac{\Delta k}{k} \times 100 \%=2 \times 2+1=5 \%$
$\therefore \quad$ So, percentage error in $k$ is $5 \%$.
subject: Physics

question: Two resistors $A$ and $B$ have values $(3.0 \pm 0.1)k\Omega $ and $(9.0 \pm 0.3)k\Omega $ respectively. If they are connected in parallel, the percentage error in the equivalent resistance is
explanation:
${A = 3.0k\,\Omega ,\Delta A = 0.1k\,\Omega }$
${B = 9.0k\,\Omega ,\Delta B = 0.3k\,\Omega }$
Now, equivalent parallel resistance $R_{P}$ is given by,
${\frac{1}{{{R_p}}} = \frac{1}{{\,A}} + \frac{1}{{\,B}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (1)}$
${\therefore \quad {R_p} = \frac{{AB}}{{A + B}} = \frac{{3 \times 9}}{{3 + 9}}}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {R_p} = 2.25\,k\,\Omega }$
Differentiating equation (1), we get,
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{ - \Delta {R_P}}}{{R_p^2}} = \frac{{ - \Delta A}}{{{A^2}}} - \frac{{\Delta B}}{{{B^2}}}}$
${\therefore \quad \Delta {R_P} = \Delta A{{\left( {\frac{{{R_P}}}{{\,A}}} \right)}^2} + \Delta B{{\left( {\frac{{{R_P}}}{B}} \right)}^2}}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = (0.1){{\left( {\frac{{2.25}}{{3.0}}} \right)}^2} + (0.3){{\left( {\frac{{2.25}}{{9.0}}} \right)}^2}}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = 0.05625 + 0.01875}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \Delta {R_P} = 0.075\,\,k\Omega }$
$\therefore$ Percentage error in equivalent resistance is,
${\frac{{\Delta {R_P}}}{{{R_P}}} \times 100 = \frac{{0.075}}{{2.25}} \times 100}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = 3.33\% }$
subject: Physics

question: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum is $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{L}{g}} .$ Measured value of  $L$ is $20.0\,cm$ known to $1\,mm$ accuracy and time for 100 oscillations of the pendulum is found to be $80\,s$ using a wrist watch of  $1\,s$ accuracy. What will be the accuracy   in the determination of  $g$ ?
explanation:
Time period, $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{L}{g}} $
${\therefore g = 4{\pi ^2}\frac{L}{{{T^2}}}}$
${\therefore \quad \frac{{\Delta g}}{g} \times 100 = \frac{{\Delta L}}{L} \times 100 + 2\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = \frac{{0.1}}{{20.0}} \times 100 + 2 \times \frac{1}{{80}} \times 100}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = \frac{{10}}{{20}} + \frac{{200}}{{80}} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{{20}}{8}}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = 3\% }$
subject: Physics

question: The following observations were taken for determining surface tension $\mathrm{T}$ of water by capillary method: Diameter of capillary  $(D) = 1.50 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m,$ Rise of water $(h) = 3.00 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m.$ Using  $g = 9.80\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$ and the simplified relation  $T = \frac{{rhg}}{2} \times {10^3}\,N{\rm{/}}m,$ what is the possible error  in  $(\% )$ surface tension?
explanation:
$D = 1.50 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m;h = 3.00 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
The maximum permissible error in $D$
$ = \Delta D = 0.01 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
The maximum permissible error in $h$
$ = \Delta h = 0.01 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$T = \frac{{{\rm{ }}rhg{\rm{ }}}}{2} \times {10^3}\,N{\rm{/}}m = \frac{{dhg}}{4} \times {10^3}\,N{\rm{/}}m$
$\therefore $ Relative error $\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} = \frac{{\Delta d}}{d} + \frac{{\Delta h}}{h} + \frac{{\Delta g}}{g}$
Since $g$ is given as a constant and is errorless
$\therefore$ Percentage error,
${\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100 = \frac{{\Delta d}}{d} \times 100 + \frac{{\Delta h}}{h} \times 100}$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = \left[ {\left( {\frac{{0.01 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{1.50 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} + \frac{{0.01 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{3.00 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}} \right) \times 100} \right]\% }$
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = 1\% }$
subject: Physics

question: The side of a cubical block when measured with a vernier calipers is 2.50 $cm$  . The vernier constant is 0.01 $cm$ . The maximum possible error in the area of the side of the block is
explanation:
${: a=2.50 {~cm}}$
Vernier constant ${=0.01 {~cm}}$
${\therefore}$ Maximum possible error in measurement of ${a=0.01 {~cm}}$
$ \Rightarrow Area = {a^2}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{\Delta A}}{A} = \frac{{2\Delta a}}{a}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta A = \frac{{2(0.01)}}{{(2.50)}} \times {\left( {2.50} \right)^2} = 0.5{\rm{ c}}{{\rm{m}}^2}$
Maximum possible error in measurement of area ${=0.05 {~cm}^{2}}$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: The percentage errors in the measurement of mass and speed are ${1 \%}$ and ${2 \%}$ respectively. What is the percentage error in the kinetic energy
explanation:
${K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta K}{K}=\dfrac{\Delta m}{m}+\dfrac{2 \Delta v}{v}$
${\%}$ error in ${K=(\%}$ error in ${m)+2 \times(\%}$ error in ${v)}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 1\% + 2 \times 2\% = 5\% $.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A Force ${F}$ is applied on a square plate of side ${L}$. If the percentage error in the determination of ${L}$ is $2\,\% $ and that in ${F}$ is $4\,\% $, what is the permissible error in pressure
explanation:
${P=\dfrac{F}{A}=\dfrac{F}{L^{2}}}$
${\dfrac{\Delta P}{P}=\dfrac{\Delta F}{F}+\dfrac{2 \Delta L}{L}}$
${=4 \%+2 \times 2 \%}$
${\dfrac{\Delta P}{P}=8 \%}$.
So correct option is(4)
subject: Physics

question: A spherometer has 20 threads per cm . Its circular scale has 100 divisions. The least count of the sphere is
explanation:
Here, for 20 threads per cm
Pitch ${=0.05 {~cm}}$
L.C. ${=\dfrac{\text { Pitch }}{\text { No. of division }}=\dfrac{0.05}{100} {~cm}=5 \times 10^{-6} {~m}}$
${=5 \mu {m}}$.
So correct option is(1)
subject: Physics

question: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum in the experiment is recorded as $2.63 s, 2.56 s, 2.42 s, 2.71 s$ and $2.80 s$ respectively. The average absolute error is
explanation:
Average value
$ = \frac{{2.63 + 2.56 + 2.42 + 2.71 + 2.80}}{5}$
$ = 2.62\,s$
$Now,\quad \left| {\Delta {T_1}} \right| = |2.62 - 2.63| = 0.01$
$\left| {\Delta {T_2}} \right| = |2.62 - 2.56| = 0.06$
$\left| {\Delta {T_3}} \right| = |2.62 - 2.42| = 0.20$
$\left| {\Delta {T_4}} \right| = |2.62 - 2.71| = 0.09$
$\left| {\Delta {T_5}} \right| = |2.62 - 2.80| = 0.18$
$\Delta T = \frac{{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{r}}
{\left[ {\Delta {T_1}| + |\Delta {T_2}| + |\Delta {T_3}\mid } \right.}\\
{ + \left| {\Delta {T_4}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_5}} \right|}
\end{array}} \right]}}{5}$
$ = \frac{{0.54}}{5} = 0.108$
$\Delta T \simeq 0.11s$
subject: Physics

question: Choose the incorrect statement out of the following.
explanation:
When the relative error is expressed in
percentage, we call it as percentage error.
Thus, statement (4) is incorrect while all other
statements regarding measurement of a quantity are correct.

options.text: Every measurement by any measuring instrument has some errors.
Every calculated physical quantity that is based on measured values has some errors.
A measurement can have more accuracy but less precision and vice-versa.
The percentage error is different from relative error.

subject: Physics

question: The length and breadth of a rectangular sheet have been measured by a measuring scale to an accuracy of  $0.1\,cm$. If the measured values are $35.2\,cm\,\,{\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} 21.7\,cm$, respectively, then find the relative error in the area of the sheet.
explanation:
Here, length of sheet
$l = (35.2 \pm 0.1)cm\,\,and\,\,{\rm{breadth}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{sheet}}$
$b = (21.7 \pm 0.1)cm$
$\therefore {\rm{Relative}}\,\,{\rm{error}}\,\,{\rm{in}}\,\,{\rm{surface}}\,\,{\rm{area}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{the}}\,\,{\rm{sheet,}}$
${\left( {\frac{{\Delta s}}{s}} \right)_{\max }} = \frac{{\Delta l}}{l} + \frac{{\Delta b}}{b} = \frac{{0.1}}{{35.2}} + \frac{{0.1}}{{21.7}}$
$ = 2.841 \times {10^{ - 3}} + 4.608 \times {10^{ - 3}}$
$ = 7.449 \times {10^{ - 3}} = \pm 7.45 \times {10^{ - 3}}$
subject: Physics

question: The length of a strip measured with a metre rod is $10.0\,cm$. Its width measured with a vernier calipers is $1.00\,cm$. The least count of the metre rod is $0.1\,cm$ and that of vernier calipers $0.01\,cm$. What will be error in its area?
explanation:
Area of strip $(A)=l b$
where, $l=$ length and $b=$ breadth.
The percentage error in area is given
by$\left( {\frac{{\Delta A}}{A}} \right) \times 100 = \left( {\frac{{\Delta l}}{l}} \right) \times 100 + \left( {\frac{{\Delta b}}{b}} \right) \times 100$
$ = \frac{{0.1}}{{10}} \times 100 + \frac{{0.01}}{1} \times 100 = \pm 2\% $
subject: Physics

question: $R = 65 \pm 1\,\Omega \,L,\,\,l = 5 \pm 0.1\,mm$ and  $d = 10 \pm 0.5\,mm$. Find error in calculation of resistivity.
explanation:
Given, $R=65 \Omega, \Delta R=1 \Omega$,
$l = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$,$\Delta \,l = 0.1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$,
$d = 10 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$ and $\Delta \,d = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$.
$\because$ Resistivity, $\rho=\dfrac{R A}{l}$
or $\rho=\dfrac{R \pi(d / 2)^{2}}{l}=\dfrac{\pi R d^{2}}{4 l}$
$\therefore \frac{{\Delta \rho }}{\rho } = \frac{{\Delta R}}{R} + 2\frac{{\Delta d}}{d} + \frac{{\Delta l}}{l}$
$\therefore \frac{{\Delta \rho }}{\rho } = \frac{1}{{65}} + 2\left( {\frac{{0.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{10 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} \right) + \frac{{0.1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}$
or$\frac{{\Delta \rho }}{\rho } = 0.0153 + 0.1 + 0.02$
or$\frac{{\Delta \rho }}{\rho } \approx 0.1353$
So,error in calculation of restivity is $13.5\% \approx 13\% $
subject: Physics

question: The screw gauge has least count of $0.005\,mm$ and its circular scale is divided into 100 equal divisions. What is the distance between two consecutive threads of its screw?
explanation:
Pitch ${=}$ L.C. ${\times}$ No. of divisions on circular scale
${=0.005 {~mm} \times 100}$
${=0.5 {~mm}}$
subject: Physics

question: For the pitch of a screw 0.1 cm and 200 divisions on circular scale, the least count will be
subject: Physics

question: The main scale of vernier calliper is divided into $0.5\,mm$ and its least count is $0.005\,cm$. The number of vernier divisions are
explanation:
Given, L.C. ${=\dfrac{S}{n}}$
${\therefore}$ No. of divisions on main scale ${(n)}$
${=\dfrac{S}{\text { L.C. }}=\dfrac{0.5 {~mm}}{0.05 {~mm}}=10}$
subject: Physics

question: A vernier caliper has 20 divisions on the vernier scale which coincide with 19 divisions on the main scale. The least count of the instrument is 0.1 mm . The main scale divisions are of
explanation:
Given, ${20 V=19 S \Rightarrow V=\dfrac{19}{20} S}$
$\begin{array}{r}\text { L.C. }=S-V=\dfrac{S}{20} \\S=20 \times 0.1 {~mm}=2 {~mm}\end{array}$
subject: Physics

question: The length, breadth, and thickness of a strip are ${(10.0 \pm 0.1) {cm},(1.00 \pm 0.01) {cm}}$, and ${(0.100 \pm}$ ${0.001) {cm}}$ respectively. The most probable error in its volume will be
explanation:
${V=}$ Length ${\times}$ width ${\times}$ thickness ${=l \times b \times t}$
$\frac{{\Delta V}}{V} \times 100$
$ = \frac{{\Delta l}}{l} \times 100 + \frac{{\Delta b}}{b} \times 100 + \frac{{\Delta t}}{t} \times 100$
${=\dfrac{0.1}{10} \times 100+\dfrac{0.01}{1} \times 100+\dfrac{0.001}{0.1} \times 100}$
${= \pm(0.01+0.01+0.01) \%= \pm 0.03 \%}$
subject: Physics

question: The pressure on a square plate is measured by measuring the force on the plate and the length of the sides of the plate. If the maximum error in the measurement of force and length are, respectively, ${4 \%}$ and ${2 \%}$, the maximum error in the measurement of pressure is
explanation:
Maximum percentage error in the measurement of pressure is
${\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \times 100=\dfrac{\Delta F}{F} \times 100+2 \times \dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \times 100}$
${=\dfrac{4}{100} \times 100+2 \times \dfrac{2}{100} \times 100}$
${=8 \%}$
subject: Physics

question: The length of a cylinder is measured with a metre rod, having least count of $0.1\,cm$. Its diameter is measured with Vernier callipers having least count of $0.1\,cm$. Given that, length is $5.0\,cm$ and radius is $2.0\,cm$. The percentage error in the calculated value of the volume will be
explanation:
Percentage error
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \times 100=\left[\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}+\dfrac{2 \Delta d}{d}\right] \times 100}$
${=\left[\dfrac{0.1}{5.0}+\dfrac{2 \times 0.01}{2.00}\right] \times 100=3 \%}$
subject: Physics

question: A physical quantity ${Q}$ is calculated according to the expression ${Q=\dfrac{A^{3} B^{3}}{C \sqrt{D}}}$. If percentage errors in ${A, B, C}$, and ${D}$ are ${2 \%, 1 \%, 3 \%}$ and ${4 \%}$, respectively, then maximum percentage errors in ${Q}$ is
explanation:
${\dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q}=\dfrac{3 \Delta A}{A}+\dfrac{3 \Delta B}{B}+\dfrac{\Delta C}{C}+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta D}{D}}$
$= \pm\left(3 \times 2+3 \times 1+3+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4\right) \%= \pm 14 \%$
subject: Physics

question: The radius of a sphere is ${(5.3 \pm 0.1) {cm}}$. The percentage error in its volume is
explanation:
${\because V=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}}$
${\because \%}$ error in volume ${=3 \times \%}$ error in radius
${=\dfrac{3 \times 0.1}{5.3} \times 100}$
subject: Physics

question: According to Joule's law of heating, heat produced ${H=I^{2} R t}$, where ${I}$ is current, ${R}$ is resistance, and ${t}$ is time. If the errors in the measurement of ${I, R}$, and ${t}$ are ${3 \%, 4 \%}$, and ${6 \%}$, respectively, then error in the measurement of ${H}$ is
explanation:
${H=I^{2} R t}$
${\therefore \dfrac{\Delta H}{H} \times 100=\left(\dfrac{2 \Delta I}{I}+\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}+\dfrac{\Delta t}{t}\right) \times 100}$
${= \pm(2 \times 3+4+6) \%= \pm 16 \%}$
subject: Physics

question: A physical quantity ${X}$ is given by ${X=\dfrac{2 k^{3} l^{2}}{m \sqrt{n}}}$The percentage error in the measurements of ${k, l, m}$ and ${n}$ are ${1 \%, 2 \%, 3 \%}$ and ${4 \%}$, respectively. The value of ${X}$ is uncertain by
explanation:
${X=\dfrac{2 k^{3} l^{2}}{m \sqrt{n}}}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta X}{X}=\dfrac{3 \Delta k}{k}+\dfrac{2 \Delta l}{l}+\dfrac{\Delta m}{m}+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta n}{n}}$
${\Rightarrow \%}$ uncertanity
${=3(A)+2(B)+3(C)+\dfrac{1}{2}(D)=12 \%}$
subject: Physics

question: In a vernier callipers, ${(N+1)}$ divisions of vernier scale coincide with ${N}$ divisions of main scale. If 1 MSD represents $0.1\,mm$, the vernier constant (in $cm$) is:
subject: Physics

question: A physical quantity $P$ is given as, $P=\dfrac{a^{2} b^{3}}{c \sqrt{d}}$. The percentage error in the measurement of $a, b, c$ and $d$ are $1 \%, 2 \%, 3 \%$ and $4 \%$ respectively. The percentage error in the measurement of quantity will be
explanation:
$P=\dfrac{a^{2} b^{3}}{C \sqrt{d}}$
Percentage error of the given physical quantity is calculated below
$\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \times 100=\dfrac{2 \Delta a}{a} \times 100+\dfrac{3 \Delta b}{b} \times 100+$
$\dfrac{\Delta c}{c} \times 100+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta d}{d} \times 100$
$=\left(2(1)+3(2)+(3)+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4\right) \times 100$
$=(2+6+3+2) \times 100$
$=13 \%$.
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: In an experiment with vernier calipers of least count $0.1\,mm$, when two jaws are joined together the zero of vernier scale lies right to the zero of the main scale and ${6^{th{\rm{ }}}}$ division of vernier scale coincides with the main scale division. While measuring the diameter of a spherical bob, the zero of vernier scale lies in between $3.22\,cm$ and $3.3\,cm$ marks, and ${4^{th{\rm{ }}}}$ division of vernier scale coincides with the main scale division. The diameter of bob is measured as
explanation:
As per question $LC = 0.1\,mm$
Positive zero Error $ = 6 \times LC = 0.6\,mm$
Reading $=\mathrm{MSR}+($ VSR $\times$ LC $)-$ Positive zero Error
$ = [32\,mm + (0.1)4\,mm] - 0.6\,mm$
$ = 31.8\,mm = 3.18\,cm$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A cylindrical wire of mass $(0.4 \pm 0.01) g$ has length $(8 \pm 0.04)cm$ and radius $(6 \pm 0.03)mm$. The maximum error in its density will be
explanation:
Given, mass of cylinder, $m = (0.4 \pm 0.01)g$
Length, $l = (8 \pm 0.04)cm$
Radius, $r = (6 \pm 0.03)mm$
Density, $d=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\pi r^{2} l}$
$\therefore$ Maximum error in density, using error formula is
$\dfrac{\Delta d}{d}=\left(\dfrac{\Delta m}{m}+2 \dfrac{\Delta r}{r}+\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}\right)$
$=\left(\dfrac{0.01}{0.4}+2 \times \dfrac{0.03}{6}+\dfrac{0.04}{8}\right)$
$=\left(\dfrac{1}{40}+\dfrac{1}{100}+\dfrac{1}{200}\right)=0.04$
Percentage error in density $=4 \%$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A body of mass $(5 \pm 0.5)kg$ is moving with a velocity of $(20 \pm 0.4)m{\rm{/}}s$. Its kinetic energy will be
explanation:
Kinetic energy,
$K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 20 \times 20=1000 J$
We can find the error as follows
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta K}{K}=\dfrac{\Delta m}{m}+\dfrac{2 \Delta v}{v}$
$=\dfrac{0.5}{5}+2 \times \dfrac{0.4}{20}=0.1+0.04$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta K = (0.14) \times 1000 = 140\,J$
$\therefore K^{\prime}=K \pm \Delta K=(1000 \pm 140) J$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In Vernier Calliper if positive zero error exists, then while taking measurements, the reading taken will be more than the actual reading.

Reason: The zero error in Vernier Calliper might have happened due to manufacturing defect or due to rough handling.
explanation: When zero of Vernier scale lies on the right side of zero of main scale, then there will be positive zero error. So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The percentage change in time period is $1.5 \%$ if the length of simple pendulum increases by $3 \%$.

Reason: Time period is directly proportional to length of pendulum.
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum of length $l$ is
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} $
$ \Rightarrow T \propto \sqrt l \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \therefore \frac{{\Delta T}}{T} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\Delta l}}{l}$
$\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 = 1.5\% $
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: When percentage errors in the measurement of mass and velocity are $1 \%$ and $2 \%$ respectively, the percentage error in kinetic $\operatorname{energy}\left(E_{k}\right)$ is $5 \%$.

Reason: $\dfrac{\Delta E_{k}}{E_{k}}=\dfrac{\Delta m}{m}+\dfrac{2 \Delta v}{v}$
explanation:
Kinetic energy $E_{k}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
The dfractional change in $E_{k}$ is:
$\frac{{\Delta {E_k}}}{{{E_k}}} = \frac{{\Delta m}}{m} + 2\frac{{\Delta v}}{v}$
$\frac{{\Delta {E_k}}}{{{E_k}}} \times 100 = \frac{{\Delta m}}{m} \times 100 + 2\frac{{\Delta v}}{v} \times 100$
$ = 1\% + 2(2 \times 2\% ) = 5\% $
So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Out of three measurements $l=0.7 \mathrm{~m} ; l=0.70 \mathrm{~m}$ and $l=0.700 \mathrm{~m}$, the last one is most accurate.

Reason: In every measurement, only the last significant digit is not accurately known.
explanation: The last number is most accurate because it has maximum significant figures only the last significant digit has uncertainty. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: If percentage error in measuring radius of a sphere is $\pm 1 \%$, the error in its volume would be $\pm 3 \%$.

Reason: $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=3\left(\dfrac{\Delta r}{r}\right)$
explanation:
As $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}$
$\therefore \frac{{\Delta V}}{V} = 3\left( {\frac{{\Delta r}}{r}} \right) = 3( \pm 1\% ) = \pm 3\% $
So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The watches having hour hand, minute hand and second hand have least count as $1\,s$.

Reason: Least count is the maximum measurement that can be measured accurately by an instrument.
explanation: The least count of a watch with hour, minute, and second hand is 1 second. Least count is actually the smallest possible measurement that can be made by the instrument. So, the assertion is true, but the reason is false. So correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A physical quantity $P$ is described by the relation $P=a^{1 / 2} b^{2} c^{3} d^{-4}$. If the relative errors in the measurement of $a, b,  c$ and $d$ respectively, are $2 \%, 1 \%, 3 \%$ and $5 \%$, then the relative error in $P$ will be
explanation:
$P=a^{1 / 2} b^{2} c^{3} d^{-4}$
The maximum possible relative error in $P$ is
$\frac{{\Delta P}}{P} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\Delta a}}{a} + 2\frac{{\Delta b}}{b} + 3\frac{{\Delta c}}{c} + 4\frac{{\Delta d}}{d}$
$\frac{{\Delta P}}{P} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{2} \times 2\% + 2 \times 1\% + $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,3 \times 3\% + 4 \times 5\% $
$\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \times 100 \%=32 \%$
subject: Physics

question: Calculate the mean percentage error in five observations, $80.0,80.5,81.0,81.5$ and 82 .
explanation:
Mean of the five observations,
$\mu=\dfrac{80.0+80.5+81.0+81.5+82}{5}$
$=\dfrac{405.0}{5}=81$
$\therefore$ Mean Error
$ = \frac{{\left[ \begin{array}{l} |80 - \mu | + |80.5 - \mu | + |81.0 - \mu | + \\ |81.5 - \mu | + |82 - \mu | \end{array} \right]}}{5}$
$\frac{{\left[ \begin{array}{l} |80 - 81| + |80.5 - 81| + |81.0 - 81| + \\ |81.5 - 81| + |82 - 81| \end{array} \right]}}{5}$
$=\dfrac{1+0.5+0+0.5+1}{5}=\dfrac{3}{5}=0.6$
$\therefore$ Mean $\%$ error $=\dfrac{0.6}{81} \times 100 \%=0.74 \%$
subject: Physics

question: In an experiment to measure, the height of a bridge by dropping stone into water underneath. If the error in measurement of time is $0.2 s$ at the end of $4 s$, then the error in estimation of height of bridge will be (neglect the water resistance, i.e. thrust)
explanation:
We know that, $s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
or $h=0(t)+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times(4)^{2}\left(\begin{array}{l}\because a=g \\ \because u=0\end{array}\right)$
$ = 78.4\,m$
Given, $\Delta t=0.2 s$
$t=4 s$
Now, for error,
$\dfrac{\Delta h}{h}= \pm 2\left(\dfrac{\Delta t}{t}\right)$
$= \pm 2\left(\dfrac{0.2}{4}\right)= \pm 0.1$
or $\Delta h= \pm 0.1 \times h$
$= \pm 0.1 \times 78.4$
$ = \pm 7.84\,m$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The error in the measurement of radius of the sphere is $0.3 \%$. The permissible error in its surface area is $0.6 \%$.

Reason: The permissible error is calculated by the formula $\dfrac{\Delta A}{A}=\dfrac{4 \Delta r}{r}$.
explanation:
Surface area of sphere, $A=4 \pi r^{2}$
(error will not be involved in constant $4 \pi$ )
Fractional error, $\dfrac{\Delta A}{A}=\dfrac{2 \Delta r}{r}$
$\dfrac{\Delta A}{A} \times 100=2 \times 0.3 \%=0.6 \%$
but $\dfrac{\Delta A}{A}=\dfrac{4 \Delta r}{r}$ is false.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and reason are correct.

subject: Physics

question: The true length of a wire is $3.678\,cm$. When the length of this wire is measured using instrument $A$, the length of the wire is $3.5\,cm$. When the length of the wire is measured using instrument $B$, it is found to have length $3.38\,cm$. Then the
explanation:
True length $ = 3.678\,cm$.
The value 3.5 is closer to 3.678 than the value $3.38 \Rightarrow A$ is more accurate. However, the value 3.38 has two decimal places.
$\Rightarrow B$ is more precise.
Correct option is (4).

options.text: measurement with $B$ is more accurate and precise.
measurement with $A$ is more precise while measurement with $B$ is more accurate.
measurement with $A$ is more accurate and precise.
measurement with $A$ is more accurate while measurement with $B$ is more precise.

subject: Physics

question:
A metal wire has mass $(0.4 \pm 0.002)g$, radius
$(0.3 \pm 0.001)mm$ and length$(5 \pm 0.02)cm$.
The maximum possible percentage error in the measurement of density will nearly be:
explanation:
$\rho=\dfrac{M}{V}=\dfrac{M}{\pi r^{2} l}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}=\dfrac{\Delta M}{M}+\dfrac{2 \Delta r}{r}+\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \%=\left[\dfrac{0.002}{0.4}+\dfrac{2(0.001)}{(0.3)}+\dfrac{0.02}{5}\right] \times 100 \%$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \%+\dfrac{2}{3} \%+\dfrac{2}{5} \%=1.6 \%$
Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The errors in the measurement which arise due to unpredictable fluctuations in temperature and voltage supply are:
explanation:
Errors arise due to unpredictable fluctuation in temperature and voltage supply are random errors.
Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: The mass of a block is 26 $mg$. A student measures the volume and finds it be 25.1 $mg$, 25.2 $mg$, and 25.3 $mg$ in the first, second, third, and fourth trial, respectively. Which of the following statements is true for his measurements?
explanation: They have good precision

options.text: They are neither precise nor accurate.

subject: Physics

question: To estimate ‘$g$’ $\left( {{\mathop{\rm from}\nolimits} \,g = 4{\pi ^2}\frac{L}{{{T^2}}}} \right),$ error in measurement of $L$ is $ \pm 2\% $ and error in measurement of $T$ is $ \pm 3\% $. The error in estimated ‘$g$’ will be
explanation:
$g = 4{\pi ^2}\frac{\ell }{{{T^2}}}$
$\frac{{\Delta \ell }}{\ell } = 2\% = \pm 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} = \pm 2\% = \pm 3 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta g}}{g} = \frac{{\Delta \ell }}{\ell } + \frac{{2\Delta T}}{T} = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}} + 2 \times 3 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$ = 8 \times {10^{ - 2}} = \pm 8\% $
subject: Physics

question: The radius of a sphere is $\left( {5.3 \pm 0.1} \right)cm$. The percentage error in its volume is
explanation:
$\because \,V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}$
$\therefore \,\% $ error in volume
$ = 3 \times \% $ error in radius.
$ = \frac{{3 \times 0.1}}{{5.3}} \times 100$
subject: Physics

question: If $X = \frac{A}{B}$ and $\Delta X,\Delta A$ and $\,\Delta B$ are maximum absolute errors in $X$, $A$ and $B$ respectively, then the maximum fractional error in $X$ is given by
explanation: When two quantities are divided, their maximum fractional or relative errors are added up hence: $\frac{{\Delta X}}{X} = \frac{{\Delta A}}{A}\, + \frac{{\Delta B}}{B}$
subject: Physics

question: The mean absolute error in the time period of 	second’s pendulum is 0.05 $s$. To express maximum estimate of error, the time period should be written as
explanation:
Mean time period $T = 2.00\,\sec $
Mean absolute error $\Delta T = 0.05\,\sec .$
To express maximum estimate of error, the time period should be written as $\left( {2.00 \pm 0.05} \right)\sec .$
subject: Physics

question: The decrease in percentage error
subject: Physics

question: What is the relation between pitch and least count of a screw gauge?
subject: Physics

question: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum is $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{L}{g}} .$ Measured value of $L$ is 20.0 $cm$ known to 4 $mm$ accuracy and time Period  of the pendulum is found to be 1 $s$ using a wrist watch of accuracy $0.005$. The accuracy in the determination of $g$ is:
explanation:
$g = 4{\pi ^2} \cdot \frac{1}{{{T^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta g}}{g} \times 100 = \frac{{\Delta l}}{l} \times 100 + 2\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100$
$ = \frac{{\Delta l}}{l} \times 100 + 2 \cdot \frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100$
Given that $\Delta l = 0.4\,cm,\,\,l = 20\,cm$
$\Delta T = 0.005,\,\,T = 1\,s$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{0.4}}{{20}} \times 100 + \frac{{2 \times 0.005}}{1} \times 100 = 3\% $
subject: Physics

question:
A person measures two quantities as $A = 1.0m \pm 0.2m,$
$B = 2.0m \pm 0.2m.$ We should report correct value for $\sqrt {AB} $ as
explanation:
Given, $A = 1.0m \pm 0.2m,B = 2.0 \pm 0.2m$
Let, $Y = \sqrt {AB} = \sqrt {\left( {1.0} \right)\left( {2.0} \right)} = 1.414\,m$
Rounding off to two significant digit $Y = 1.4\,m$
$\frac{{\Delta Y}}{Y} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\frac{{\Delta A}}{A} + \frac{{\Delta B}}{B}} \right]$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\frac{{0.2}}{{1.0}} + \frac{{0.2}}{{2.0}}} \right] = \frac{{0.6}}{{2 \times 2.0}}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta Y = \frac{{0.6Y}}{{2 \times 2.0}} = \frac{{0.6 \times 1.4}}{{2 \times 20}} = 0.212$
Rounding off to one significant digit
$\Delta Y = 0.2m$
Thus, correct value for
$\sqrt {AB} = Y + \Delta Y = 1.4 \pm 0.2m$
subject: Physics

question: In a circuit, potential difference across 	resistance, is $V = \left( {4 \pm 0.25} \right)V$ and current in 	resistance, $I = \left( {1 \pm 0.1} \right)A.$ What is the value of resistance with its percentage error?
subject: Physics

question: A physicist performs an experiment and takes 200 readings. He repeats the same experiment and now takes 800 readings. By doing so

options.text: The probable error is four times
The probable error is reduced by a factor

subject: Physics

question: A student measured the diameter of a wire using a screw gauge with least count 0.001 $cm$ and listed the measurements. The correct measurement is
explanation: The measurement is correct upto third decimal. Therefore, it must be 5.320 $cm$
subject: Physics

question: The pitch of a screw gauge is 1 $mm$ and there are 100 divisions on the circular scale. In measuring the diameter of a sphere there are seven divisions on the linear scale and forty five divisions on circular scale coincides with the reference line. Find the diameter of the sphere.
explanation:
$L.C = \frac{1}{{100}} = 0.01mm$
Linear scale reading $ = 7 \times {\mathop{\rm pitch}\nolimits} = 7mm$
Circular scale reading $ = 45 \times 0.01 = 0.45\,mm$
$\therefore $ Total reading $ = (7 + 0.45) = 7.45\,mm$
subject: Physics

question: The length of a strip measured with a metre rod is 10.0 $cm$. Its width measured with a vernier callipers is 1.00 $cm$. The least count of the metre rod is 0.1 $cm$ and that of vernier calliper is 0.01 $cm$. What will be the error in its area?
explanation:
$\Delta A = \left( {\frac{{\Delta l}}{l} + \frac{{\Delta b}}{b}} \right)A$
$ = \pm \left( {\frac{{0.1}}{{10.0}} + \frac{{0.01}}{{1.00}}} \right) \times (10.0 \times 1.00)\,c{m^2}$
$ = \pm 0.02 \times 10 = \pm 0.2c{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question: One centimeter on the main scale of vernier callipers is divided into ten equal parts. If 20 divisions of vernier scale coincide with 8 small divisions of the main scale. What will be the least count of callipers ?
explanation:
$L.C = \left( {\frac{{b - a}}{b}} \right)M = \left( {\frac{{20 - 8}}{{20}}} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{10}}} \right)cm$
$ = \frac{3}{{50}}cm = 0.06\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum in the experiment is recorded as 2.63 $s$, 2.56 $s$, 2.42 $s$, 2.71 $s$ and 2.80 $s$ respectively. The average absolute error is:
explanation:
Average value
$ = \frac{{2.63 + 2.56 + 2.42 + 2.71 + 2.80}}{5} = 2.62s$
Now,
$\left| {\Delta {T_1}} \right| = 2.63 \sim 2.62 = 0.01$
$\left| {\Delta {T_2}} \right| = 2.56 \sim 2.62 = 0.06$
$\left| {\Delta {T_3}} \right| = 2.42 \sim 2.62 = 0.20$
$\left| {\Delta {T_4}} \right| = 2.71 \sim 2.62 = 0.09$
$\left| {\Delta {T_5}} \right| = 2.80 \sim 2.62 = 0.18$
$\Delta T = \frac{{\left| {\Delta {T_1}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_2}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_3}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_4}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_5}} \right|}}{5}$
$ = \frac{{0.54}}{5} = 0.108 \simeq 0.11s$
subject: Physics

question: A thin copper wire of length $L$ increases in length by 3% when heated from  ${T_1}$ to ${T_2}$. If a copper cube having side 10 $L$ is heated from  ${T_1}$ to ${T_2}$, what will be the percentage change in area of one face of the cube?
explanation:
Area $A = 10L \times 10L = 100{L^2}$
Fractional change in the area is
$\frac{{\Delta A}}{A} = 2\frac{{\Delta L}}{L}$
Percentage change in area
$ = \frac{{\Delta A}}{A} \times 100 = 2 \times \frac{{\Delta L}}{L} \times 100 = 2 \times 3\% = 6\% $
subject: Physics

question: The least count of a stop watch is 1/5 second. The time of 20 oscillations of a pendulum is measured to be 25 seconds. How much will be the percentage error in the measurement of time ?
explanation:
Error in measuring 25 sec,
$ = \frac{1}{5}\sec . = 0.2\sec .$
$\therefore $ Percentage error $ = \frac{{0.2}}{{25}} \times 100 = 0.8\% $
subject: Physics

question: A circular park has a radius of $(12.1\,m\, \pm \,0.1\,m)$. Find the area of the park with percentage error.
explanation:
$r = 12.1\,m\, \pm \,0.1\,m$
${\mathop{\rm Area}\nolimits} {\rm{ = }}\pi {{\rm{r}}^{\rm{2}}} = \pi {(12.1)^2} = 460\,{m^2}$
Percentage error,
$\frac{{\Delta A}}{A} = 2\frac{{\Delta r}}{r} = 2 \times \frac{{0.1}}{{12.1}} \times 100 = 1.65\% $
$\therefore $ Area $ = 460\,{m^2} \pm \,1.65\,\% $
subject: Physics

question: An object covers $(16.0 \pm 0.4)m$ distance in $(4.0 \pm 0.2)s$. Find out its speed.
explanation:
Speed $v = $ $\frac{{{\rm{distance}}}}{{{\rm{time}}}}$ $ = \frac{{16.0}}{{4.0}} = 4.0{\mkern 1mu} \,m/s;$
Error in speed
$\Delta v = \pm \left( {\frac{{\Delta s}}{s} + \frac{{\Delta t}}{t}} \right)v = \left( {\frac{{0.4}}{{16.0}} + \frac{{0.2}}{{4.0}}} \right)\left( {4.0} \right)$
$v = (4.0 \pm 0.3)\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: For a cubical block, error in measurement of sides is $ \pm 1\% $ and error in measurement of mass is $ \pm 2\% $, then maximum possible error in density is:
explanation:
$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{m}{{{\ell ^3}}}$
Given:$\frac{{\Delta m}}{m} = \pm 2\% = \pm 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$\frac{{\Delta \ell }}{\ell } = \pm 1\% = \pm 1 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$\frac{{\Delta \rho }}{\rho } = \frac{{\Delta m}}{m} + 3\frac{{\Delta \ell }}{\ell }$
$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}} + 3 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$ = 5 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 5\% $
subject: Physics

question: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} $ where $l$ is about 100 $cm$ and is known to have 1 $mm$ accuracy. The period is about 2 $s$. The time of 100 oscillations is measured by a stop watch of least count 0.1 sec. The percentage error in $g$ is
explanation:
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {l/g} \Rightarrow {T^2} = 4{\pi ^2}l/g \Rightarrow g = \frac{{4{\pi ^2}l}}{{{T^2}}}$
Here % error in
$l = \frac{{1\,mm}}{{100\,cm}} \times 100 = \frac{{0.1}}{{100}} \times 100 = 0.1\% $
and % error in $T = \frac{{0.1}}{{2 \times 100}} \times 100 = 0.05\% $
$\therefore \,\% $ error in $g = \,\% $ error in $l + 2$ (% error in $T$ )
$ = 0.2\,\% $
subject: Physics

question: The radius of earth is estimated with an  error of 1 %. Estimate  the error in the measurement of its volume.
explanation:
Volume, $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}$
$\therefore $ error in estimation of volume,
$\frac{{\Delta V}}{V} \times 100 = 3\frac{{\Delta r}}{r} \times 100 = 3\% $
subject: Physics

question: Estimate the percentage error in the determination of density of a material taken in the form of a cylinder with 2 % error in mass , 1 % error in radius and 3 % error in height .
explanation:
Density of material of a cylinder,
$\rho = \frac{{Mass}}{{Volume}} = \frac{M}{{\pi {r^2}h}}$
$\frac{{\Delta \rho }}{\rho } \times 100 = \left( {\frac{{\Delta M}}{M} + 2\frac{{\Delta r}}{r} + \frac{{\Delta h}}{h}} \right) \times 100$
$ = 2\% + 2 \times 1\% + 3\% = 7\% $
subject: Physics

question: The percentage of error in the measurement of mass and speed are 2% and 3% respectively. How much will be the maximum error in the estimation of the kinetic energy obtained by measuring mass and speed?
explanation:
$\therefore E = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$\therefore \,\% $ Error in $KE$
$ = \,\% $ error in mass $ + 2 \times \% $ error in velocity
$ = 2 + (2 \times 3) = 8\% $
subject: Physics

question: If $X = A \times B,\Delta X,\Delta A$ and $\Delta B$ are maximum absolute errors in $X$, $A$ and $B$ respectively, then the maximum fractional error in $X$ is given by
explanation:
When two quantities are multiplied, their maximum relative errors are added up
hence: $\frac{{\Delta X}}{X} = \frac{{\Delta A}}{A}\, + \frac{{\Delta B}}{B}$
subject: Physics
